content.text: Action execution is the layer of a computer-use agent that transforms model decisions into real operating-system events. After a vision model identifies what needs to happen on screen, a dispatcher converts that decision into the lowest-level primitives the OS understands: mouse movement, button press, keystroke sequence, scroll wheel delta, or a new screenshot request. Getting this layer right determines whether the agent can reliably complete multi-step tasks without human intervention.

attrs.alt: Action execution pipeline: screen capture, vision model, action decision, executor dispatch, verify outcome
attrs.title: Action Execution Pipeline

content.text: Action execution for computer-use agents is the runtime layer that accepts a structured action description (typically a JSON tool_use block from an LLM) and physically enacts that action on a running desktop or browser. The executor is deliberately thin: it does not plan, it does not interpret screenshots, and it does not decide what to do next. Its sole job is to faithfully produce OS-level input events and return the post-action screen state to the planning layer.

The executor handles five canonical action categories: pointer actions (move, click, double-click, right-click, drag), keyboard actions (type text, press a single key, send a key combination), scroll actions (vertical or horizontal), system actions (take a screenshot, focus a window, wait), and clipboard actions (copy, paste). Together these cover every interaction a human can perform via mouse and keyboard.

A vision model can accurately identify where a button is on screen, but that knowledge is worthless unless the action executor can reliably move the cursor to exactly those pixel coordinates and fire the click event before the UI state changes. Execution fidelity — precise coordinates, correct timing between events, proper handling of modifier keys — separates agents that work once in a demo from agents that reliably complete tasks across diverse applications and screen resolutions.

Execution is also the primary source of failures in computer-use pipelines. Off-by-one coordinate errors, races between fast-rendering UIs and slow action dispatchers, and clipboard corruption from concurrent processes are all execution-layer problems. Robust error handling here prevents cascading failures that corrupt the agent's mental model of task progress.
Generality — a single executor implementation works across all GUI applications without application-specific plugins.
Separation of concerns — the planning model (which action?) is decoupled from the execution layer (how to send that action), making each independently testable.
Auditability — every action dispatched can be logged with pre- and post-screenshots, creating a complete replay record.
OS-native reliability — using pyautogui or xdotool calls the same input path as a real human, so the application sees identical events.
Deterministic replay — a sequence of action records can be replayed exactly for regression testing or RPA automation.
Composable with any model — the executor is model-agnostic; swap Claude for GPT-4o without changing the dispatch code.
Pixel-coordinate brittleness: coordinates from a 1920x1080 capture are wrong on a 2560x1440 display unless DPI scaling is accounted for.
Timing sensitivity: typing too fast causes dropped characters in some applications; hardcoded sleeps are fragile under load.
No semantic understanding: the executor cannot detect that a click landed on the wrong element — it blindly executes coordinates.
Screenshot latency: capturing, encoding, and transmitting a screenshot adds 200-800 ms to every step, limiting throughput.
Platform divergence: pyautogui behaves differently on macOS (Accessibility permission), Windows (UAC), and Linux (Wayland vs. X11).
Clipboard interference: global clipboard state can be corrupted by other running applications during type or paste actions.
Invisible elements: browser extensions, system overlays, and hardware-accelerated video surfaces do not appear in screenshot captures.

At the center of the executor is an action dispatch loop. The loop receives a list of tool_use content blocks from the model response, iterates through them in order, calls the appropriate OS API for each block type, waits a configurable settle time, and then captures a fresh screenshot that becomes the tool_result fed back to the model.

The action schema is defined by the computer-use tool specification. For Anthropic's API the computer tool produces actions with a top-level action field (screenshot, left_click, right_click, double_click, type, key, scroll, drag) plus coordinate, text, key, and direction fields depending on action type. The executor switches on action and calls the matching pyautogui or subprocess primitive.

Settle time is a configurable delay inserted after every action before the next screenshot. A value of 300-500 ms covers most UI transitions. For actions that trigger animations or network requests, an explicit wait_for_stable_screen call using perceptual hashing detects when rendering has settled rather than sleeping a fixed duration.

The pipeline runs as a loop: screenshot capture feeds a vision model which emits tool_use blocks, the executor dispatches each block and returns a fresh screenshot as tool_result, and the loop continues until the model emits only text (task complete) or max_steps is reached. The reliability handling box in the diagram intercepts common failures — missing elements, unexpected dialogs, slow renders — before they reach the model as confusing context.
Automating desktop applications with no programmatic API (legacy ERP, imaging software, CAD tools).
End-to-end web workflows that span multiple tabs and authentication flows.
Testing GUI applications from the user perspective, complementing unit and integration tests.
Data entry tasks that require navigating complex multi-page forms.
Screen-scraping workflows where the data source has no API and is rendered in a browser.
Creating AI assistants that can operate any application on behalf of a user.
Install dependencies: pyautogui for cross-platform input dispatch, Pillow for screenshot capture, imagehash for stable-screen detection, and the Anthropic SDK for the model loop.

pip install anthropic pyautogui pillow imagehash
# Linux only (X11):
apt-get install xdotool scrot

Implement a screenshot_b64 helper that captures the screen and returns a base64-encoded PNG. Implement dispatch_action that switches on the action type. Wrap both in the agent loop shown in the Examples section. Add a wait_for_stable_screen guard for actions that trigger slow UI transitions.

def run_agent_loop(task: str, max_steps: int = 20) -> str:
    """
    Core agentic loop: capture -> ask Claude -> dispatch -> repeat.
    Returns a summary of what the agent accomplished.
    """
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]

    for step in range(max_steps):
        # 1. Capture current screen state
        img_b64 = screenshot_b64()
        screen_msg = {
            "role": "user",
            "content": [
                {"type": "image",
                 "source": {"type": "base64", "media_type": "image/png", "data": img_b64}},
                {"type": "text", "text": "Current screen. Continue working on the task."}
            ]
        }
        messages.append(screen_msg)

        # 2. Ask the model what to do next
        response = client.beta.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=[{"type": "computer_20250124", "name": "computer",
                    "display_width_px": 1920, "display_height_px": 1080}],
            messages=messages,
            betas=["computer-use-2025-01-24"],
        )

        # 3. Collect text + tool_use blocks
        assistant_content = response.content
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_content})

        tool_uses = [b for b in assistant_content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        if not tool_uses:
            # Model stopped without actions — task done or stuck
            text_blocks = [b.text for b in assistant_content if hasattr(b, "text")]
            return " ".join(text_blocks)

        # 4. Dispatch every action; build tool_result for next turn
        tool_results = []
        for tu in tool_uses:
            dispatch_action(tu.input)
            # Fresh screenshot becomes the result
            result_img = screenshot_b64()
            tool_results.append({
                "type": "tool_result",
                "tool_use_id": tu.id,
                "content": [{"type": "image",
                             "source": {"type": "base64",
                                        "media_type": "image/png",
                                        "data": result_img}}]
            })
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    return "max_steps reached"

import subprocess, re, time

def wait_for_window(title_fragment: str, timeout: float = 10.0) -> bool:
    """
    Poll xdotool until a window whose title contains title_fragment
    appears, or until timeout seconds elapse.
    """
    deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout
    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["xdotool", "search", "--name", title_fragment],
            capture_output=True, text=True
        )
        if result.returncode == 0 and result.stdout.strip():
            return True
        time.sleep(0.25)
    return False

def focus_window_by_title(title_fragment: str) -> None:
    """Bring a window to the foreground and activate keyboard focus."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["xdotool", "search", "--name", title_fragment],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    wid = result.stdout.strip().splitlines()[-1]  # last match
    subprocess.run(["xdotool", "windowactivate", "--sync", wid], check=True)

def type_in_window(title_fragment: str, text: str) -> None:
    """Focus a window by title, then type text into it reliably."""
    focus_window_by_title(title_fragment)
    time.sleep(0.15)
    subprocess.run(
        ["xdotool", "type", "--clearmodifiers", "--delay", "40", text],
        check=True
    )

import pyautogui, time
from PIL import Image, ImageGrab
import imagehash

def screen_hash() -> str:
    """Compute a perceptual hash of the current screen for change detection."""
    img = ImageGrab.grab()
    return str(imagehash.phash(img))

def wait_for_stable_screen(max_wait: float = 5.0, stable_frames: int = 3) -> bool:
    """
    Wait until the screen stops changing — useful after clicking a button
    that triggers animations or heavy loading.
    """
    last_hash = screen_hash()
    stable_count = 0
    deadline = time.monotonic() + max_wait
    while time.monotonic() < deadline:
        time.sleep(0.3)
        current = screen_hash()
        if current == last_hash:
            stable_count += 1
            if stable_count >= stable_frames:
                return True
        else:
            stable_count = 0
            last_hash = current
    return False  # timed out — caller decides how to handle

def click_with_verify(x: int, y: int, expected_change: bool = True) -> bool:
    """Click a coordinate and verify the screen changed (or did not)."""
    before = screen_hash()
    pyautogui.click(x, y)
    wait_for_stable_screen()
    after = screen_hash()
    changed = before != after
    if expected_change and not changed:
        print(f"WARNING: click at ({x},{y}) produced no visual change")
    return changed

A team at a mid-size insurance company needed to extract structured data from a legacy claims application that had no API and was built on WinForms. The application ran only on Windows and required a three-step authentication flow followed by navigating a tree of nested menus to find each claim record. They built a computer-use agent using Claude claude-opus-4-5 with a custom executor that handled DPI scaling (the app ran at 125% on a 4K monitor) and inserted explicit wait_for_stable_screen calls after each tree node expansion. The agent processed 3,000 claim records per day with a 94% end-to-end success rate, falling back to a human review queue for the remaining 6% where the screen state differed from the expected layout.
Hardcoding screen coordinates from a single resolution — always resolve coordinates relative to the model's screenshot size and scale to the actual display.
Missing settle time after typing — applications with autocomplete or validation logic may take 100-300 ms to update state after the last keystroke.
Treating screenshot as synchronous — on a busy machine, capture + encode can take 500 ms; do not assume the screen is current the moment capture returns.
Ignoring DPI scaling — pyautogui moves the cursor in physical pixels; if the screenshot was captured at logical resolution, multiply coordinates by the scale factor.
Over-clicking — clicking a button that is already active or a toggle that is already on produces an unexpected state flip.
Not handling modal dialogs — a system save dialog or permission prompt can block all subsequent actions until dismissed.
Using global clipboard for type — paste via clipboard fails if the clipboard is overwritten by another process between copy and paste.
Always verify the post-action screen before proceeding: compare a hash of the pre-action and post-action frames to confirm the action had visible effect.
Use xdotool type --clearmodifiers on Linux instead of pyautogui.typewrite to avoid modifier key leakage in multi-step sessions.
Set FAILSAFE = True in pyautogui so moving the cursor to the top-left corner aborts runaway automation.
Add jitter (small random offsets) to click coordinates to avoid detection by applications that fingerprint perfectly-round pixel positions.
Log every action with its coordinates, timestamp, and pre/post screenshot hash to enable post-hoc debugging.
Cap max_steps per task to prevent infinite loops when the agent gets stuck in a UI cycle.
Use coordinate normalization: store coordinates as fractions of screen width/height so recordings replay correctly at different resolutions.

The main bottleneck in the execution loop is screenshot capture and model inference. Capture + PNG encode takes 50-200 ms on a modern machine. The vision model (Claude claude-opus-4-5 with computer-use) adds 1-4 s per step depending on output token count. A typical agent loop runs at 1-3 steps per second, meaning a 50-step task takes 30-180 seconds.

To improve throughput: reduce screenshot resolution (800x600 captures encode 4x faster than 1920x1080 and still contain enough pixel detail for element identification), use streaming responses to overlap model inference with executor dispatch, and parallelize independent subtasks across multiple agent instances each controlling a separate virtual display (Xvfb).
Most common; needs Accessibility on macOS
More reliable for keystrokes; no Wayland
Scrot faster than Pillow ImageGrab on X11
Avoids pyautogui typewrite for long text
Reliable for web; blind to non-DOM overlays
UI Automation — more stable than pixel coords
An action executor that blindly fires OS input events is a significant security surface. Key controls to put in place:
Sandbox the agent process — run it in a Docker container or VM so a runaway agent cannot affect the host file system.
Restrict the pyautogui FAILSAFE and add a watchdog thread that kills the process if no model response arrives within a timeout.
Audit log every action with its screenshot context; retain logs for post-hoc review of any unintended side effects.
Never pass raw user input as typewrite text without sanitizing — an adversarial task description could inject keyboard shortcuts.
Use a dedicated low-privilege OS user account for the agent so elevated-privilege dialogs (UAC prompts) fail rather than silently proceeding.
Rate-limit API calls to prevent runaway cost from loops that keep triggering model inference without making progress.
Action execution translates model decisions into OS-level input events — mouse clicks, keystrokes, scroll, and screenshot.
The core loop is: capture screen → call model → dispatch tool_use actions → capture post-action screen → repeat.
Timing and DPI handling are the most common sources of execution failures in production deployments.
Stable-screen detection with perceptual hashing is more reliable than fixed sleep delays for waiting for UI transitions.
Log every action with coordinates and screenshot hashes to enable debugging of multi-step task failures.
Sandbox the agent process and use a low-privilege account to limit the blast radius of any runaway execution.
The executor is model-agnostic: the same dispatch code works with any vision model that emits structured tool_use actions.
